whatItDoes: Generates ULIDs (Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifiers) — 26-character identifiers encoded in Crockford Base32 that sort chronologically. Supports generating up to 100 ULIDs at once with timestamp component highlighting.
whyItExists: UUIDs are not sortable by creation time and use inefficient encoding. ULIDs provide a sortable, URL-friendly alternative that embeds the timestamp, making them ideal for database primary keys and distributed systems.
whoShouldUse: Backend developers choosing identifiers for databases, distributed systems engineers, and anyone needing time-sortable unique IDs.
useCases: Generate unique, time-sortable primary keys for database records
Create unique identifiers for distributed systems that need lexicographic ordering
Replace UUIDv4 with a sortable, more compact identifier format
Generate multiple ULIDs for batch inserts or test data
Extract the timestamp from a ULID to determine when it was created
instructions: Set the count (1-100) of ULIDs to generate
Each ULID is displayed with the timestamp portion highlighted in a different color
Click 'Copy' next to any individual ULID, or 'Copy All' to copy every ULID on separate lines
The timestamp portion (first 10 characters) encodes the millisecond-precision creation time
examples: Timestamp portion: 01ARZ3NDEK (first 10 chars, sortable by time)
bestPractices: Use ULIDs when you need both uniqueness and chronological sorting in your identifiers
ULIDs are 128 bits — they provide the same collision resistance as UUIDv4
The Crockford Base32 encoding is case-insensitive and excludes ambiguous characters (I, L, O, U)
Sort ULIDs as strings — they maintain chronological order lexicographically
commonMistakes: Expecting ULIDs to be exactly 32 characters — they are 26 characters long
Confusing ULIDs with UUIDs — ULIDs are not formatted with hyphens
Using ULIDs in a field that expects UUID format — they are not compatible
Assuming the random portion provides additional time information — only the first 10 chars are timestamp
faq: What is a ULID? A Universally Unique Lexicographically Sortable Identifier — a 128-bit value encoded as 26 Crockford Base32 characters.
How does ULID sorting work? ULIDs sort lexicographically in time order because the timestamp occupies the most significant bits.
Are ULIDs collision-resistant? Yes, the 80-bit random component provides collision resistance comparable to UUIDv4.
Can I extract the timestamp from a ULID? Yes, the first 10 characters encode the millisecond-precision Unix timestamp.
